critique.summary: The article explains POSSE (Publish on your Own Site, Syndicate Elsewhere), a content syndication model where users publish content first on their own website and then share copies or links on third-party platforms. It highlights the benefits of ownership, reduced dependence on third-party services, better searchability, and maintaining control over content. The article also discusses implementation details, examples, related concepts, and the evolution of POSSE within the IndieWeb community.
mainPoints: POSSE involves publishing content on one's own site first, then syndicating copies or links to social media or other third-party platforms.
This approach ensures ownership of content, canonical URLs, and reduces dependence on third-party services that might impose restrictions or go offline.
POSSE copies link back to the original post, improving discovery, search engine ranking, and combating spam.
The article provides examples of POSSE implementations across various platforms like Twitter, Facebook, Medium, WordPress, and Ghost.
POSSE contrasts with other syndication models like PESOS (post elsewhere, syndicate to own site) and COPE (create once, publish everywhere without owning the original).
The article also covers technical aspects, software tools, publishing flows, and community examples of POSSE adoption.
It discusses challenges like updating or deleting syndicated copies and the importance of linking back to originals for SEO and content integrity.
critique.truthfulness: The article accurately describes the POSSE concept as defined and promoted by the IndieWeb community, a well-documented and established approach to content syndication. The benefits and challenges outlined align with known practices in web publishing and content ownership. The examples and references to software tools and community implementations are consistent with publicly available information. The distinctions made between POSSE, PESOS, COPE, and PESETAS reflect recognized syndication models. The article is comprehensive and factual, though it primarily reflects the perspective and terminology of the IndieWeb movement.
considerations: The article does not deeply address the potential technical complexity and maintenance overhead for average users implementing POSSE.
It assumes a level of web development knowledge that may not be accessible to all content creators.
The impact of platform API restrictions (e.g., Twitter's recent API changes) on POSSE implementations is mentioned but could be explored further.
The article does not extensively discuss privacy or security implications of syndicating content across multiple platforms.
There is limited discussion on how POSSE fits into the broader ecosystem of decentralized social media and federated networks beyond IndieWeb.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the POSSE (Publish on your Own Site, Syndicate Elsewhere) approach, with users sharing their experiences, challenges, and perspectives on content ownership, automation difficulties, and community engagement. Some emphasize the value of owning content and controlling distribution, while others note the impersonal or spammy feel of cross-posting and the difficulties in scaling comment sections.

comments.author: nicbou
comments.text:
I follow this approach. It's mostly because I want to own the land I build on.

It works well, but it's hard to automate. In the end you must manually cross-post, and both the post and the discussion will vary by community. You end up being active in multiple different communities and still getting little traffic from the effort.

It's not such a great way to drive traffic. On the other hand, it's a wonderful way to work in public.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately highlights the core benefits and challenges of POSSE, emphasizing content ownership and the manual effort required, which aligns with common experiences.

comments.author: rednafi
comments.text:
I follow this religiously. The process of posting is manual but it works fairly well if your intention is good and you're not blog spamming in different forums.

But I intentionally haven't added a comment section to my blog [1]. Mostly because I don't get paid to write there and addressing the comments - even the good ones - requires a ton of energy.

Also, scaling the comment section is a pain. I had disqus integrated into my Hugo site but it became a mess when people started having actual discussion and the section got longer and longer.

If the write ups are any useful, it generally appears here or reddit and I often link back those discussions in the articles. That's good enough for me.

[1]: https://rednafi.com
comments.analysis: Provides a practical perspective on the manual nature of POSSE and the difficulties in managing comments, which is a common pain point for bloggers.

comments.author: taurusnoises
comments.text: As someone on the receiving end of POSSE, who is often on the multiple platforms people post to, this approach ends up feeling impersonal and spammy. I totally get the reasoning people have for doing it. But, to me, it's very "ship it" focused, rather than conversation focused. Maybe I'm just getting old.
comments.analysis: Offers a valuable counterpoint from a community member's perspective, highlighting the potential downsides of POSSE in terms of engagement quality.

comments.author: dewey
comments.text: I try to do that all the time, I've build a plugin for my blog framework (Kirby: https://plugins.getkirby.com/dewey/kirby-posse) that does that and cross posts it to Bluesky and Mastodon automatically.
comments.analysis: Shows practical implementation of POSSE automation, supporting the discussion on the feasibility and tools available.

comments.author: janalsncm
comments.text:
A cool feature for the small web would be:

1. I like your blog and subscribe to its RSS

2. I see new posts in my RSS reader with syndication links to (HN/reddit/twitter/etc).

3. I can go to those places to talk about it.

Low tech version is just linking to those discussions at the bottom of your post I guess.
comments.analysis: Suggests a simple, low-tech method to integrate POSSE with discussion platforms, enhancing discoverability and engagement.

comments.author: tomaytotomato
comments.text:
I've always liked this idea.

However I am not sure about "perma-shortlinks", for discovery on other sites as the means of networking and discovering content. It seems clunky to maintain as it requires a human or some automation to curate/maintain the links. If a blog removes a link to another blog, then that pathway is closed.

It would be cool if we could solve that with a "DNS for tags/topics" a - Domain Content Server (DCS) e.g.

1. tomaytotomato.com ==> publishes to the DCS of topics (tech, java, travel)

2. DCS adds domain to those topics

3. Rating or evaluating of the content on website based on those tags (not sure the mechanics here, but it could be exploited or gamed)

You could have several DCS for topics servers run by organisations or individuals.

e.g. the Lobsters DNS for topics server would be really fussy about #tech or #computerscience blog posts, and would self select for more high brow stuff

Meanwhile a more casual tech group would score content higher for Youtube content or Toms Hardware articles.

This is just spit balling.
comments.analysis: Presents an innovative idea to improve content discovery and networking beyond traditional POSSE methods, though speculative.

comments.author: MrOrelliOReilly
comments.text: I like the principle, but I also find that we software folk commonly mistake the creation of a website as the goal, rather than the production of "content" (e.g. blog posts). I spent years trying to publish a blog and continually getting derailed building the ultimate static website. Recently I switched to a Substack hosted on my own subdomain, and now I'm finally writing. At least I still own the subdomain.
comments.analysis: Highlights a common pitfall in content creation and the importance of focusing on writing rather than perfecting the website.

comments.author: cubefox
comments.text:
I wonder whether this actually works:

> Q: Do we need to worry about search engines penalizing apparently duplicate posts?

> A: That's why the POSSE copies SHOULD always link back to the originals. So that search engines can infer that the copies are just copies. Ideally POSSE copies on silos should use rel-canonical to link back to the originals, but even without explicit rel-canonical, the explicit link back to the original is a strong hint that it is an original.
comments.analysis: Accurately addresses SEO concerns related to POSSE, providing a best practice to avoid penalties for duplicate content.

comments.author: merelysounds
comments.text:
I do this, my site is in my profile description.

However, I skip permashortlinks - I try to keep my regular links relevant and short. Also, I like seeing full links, they can often indicate what content awaits there - vs short links, which are more opaque.

That's one more benefit of this workflow: it can be adjusted to fit one's personal preferences. I suppose others might prefer short links or maybe at some point I'll change my mind; with POSSE making these kind of changes is easy.
comments.analysis: Emphasizes flexibility in POSSE implementation and the benefits of tailoring link presentation to user preferences.

comments.author: benwerd
comments.text:
I love it when this shows up from time to time. Everyone should own their own content! And the indieweb community and its underlying philosophy are worth celebrating.

If you haven't, you should try to get to a Homebrew Website Club. Go talk to people about making your own, weird spot on the web that truly represents you. It'll make you feel great about technology again, I promise.
comments.analysis: Expresses strong support for content ownership and community involvement, encouraging participation in related movements.
